topics.title: ECDIS
topics.subtitle: Electronic Chart Display and Information System

content.quick_look:
A digital navigation system that replaces paper charts. It shows the ship's real-time position on an electronic chart using GPS data.

IMO made it mandatory because it reduces navigational errors, integrates multiple data sources (AIS, radar, depth), and updates charts digitally instead of manually correcting paper ones.

⚠️ Most confusing part: The difference between raster charts (scanned paper charts) and vector charts (true ENCs). Only vector ENCs count as official chart folio under SOLAS. Using raster charts without paper backup means you're non-compliant.
content.deep_dive:
ECDIS works by feeding GPS position into the system, which overlays it onto official Electronic Nautical Charts (ENCs). It constantly checks your route against safety parameters — shallow water, traffic separation schemes, restricted zones — and triggers alarms when approaching danger.

Two chart types exist:
• Vector ENCs (S-57 format) — official, layered data, objects have attributes
• Raster charts — essentially scanned paper, no intelligent data layer

Only vector ENCs satisfy SOLAS requirements as the primary means of navigation.

ECDIS alarms:
• Alarm — requires immediate action
• Warning — requires attention
• Caution — awareness only

Never disable or bypass alarms without proper logging and senior officer authorization. Every alarm dismissal is recorded by the VDR.
content.on_watch:
🚨 ECDIS ALARM RIGHT NOW?

1. DON'T dismiss it immediately — read what it says
2. Check alarm type: Anti-grounding? Cross-track? AIS conflict?
3. Anti-grounding alarm → check chart scale, zoom in, verify position against radar
4. System frozen → switch to backup ECDIS immediately, inform Master
5. Chart outdated warning → note in logbook, continue with caution

Backup paper charts location: _____ (fill in your ship)

content.quick_look:
A transponder system that continuously broadcasts your ship's identity, position, speed, and course — and receives the same from nearby vessels.

Mandatory under SOLAS for vessels above 300 GT.

⚠️ Most confusing part: AIS is NOT a collision avoidance system. Fishing boats, small craft, and military vessels often have AIS switched off. If you only watch AIS, you're blind to them.
content.deep_dive:
Class A AIS transmits position every 2–10 seconds when moving, every 3 minutes at anchor.

AIS data includes:
• MMSI (unique vessel ID)
• Position, COG, SOG, heading
• Vessel name, type, dimensions
• Destination and ETA
• Navigation status

Class A vs Class B:
• Class A — mandatory for SOLAS vessels
• Class B — used on smaller vessels, lower transmit power

AIS can be manipulated or spoofed — never trust AIS without radar confirmation for collision avoidance decisions.
content.on_watch:
🚨 AIS ISSUE RIGHT NOW?

1. Target disappeared from AIS → check on radar immediately, it's still there
2. Your AIS not transmitting → check VHF antenna, reboot transponder
3. AIS and radar don't match → trust radar, investigate AIS
4. Dangerous target with no AIS → treat as real vessel, apply COLREGS

Your MMSI: _____ (fill in your ship)

content.quick_look:
The ship's black box. Records bridge audio, radar images, navigational data, and ship parameters on a rolling 12-hour loop.

⚠️ Most confusing part: Everything you say on the bridge is recorded. Every alarm you dismiss is logged. Dismissing an alarm you didn't understand becomes evidence of negligence in an investigation.
content.deep_dive:
The VDR records:
• Bridge audio
• Radar video images
• ECDIS display
• AIS data
• Speed, heading, position
• Engine orders and responses
• All alarms — every trigger and acknowledgement

Data stored in a protective capsule surviving fire (1000°C / 1hr), sinking (6000m / 30 days), and impact.

The capsule has a ULB (Underwater Locator Beacon) that pings at 37.5 kHz for 30 days.
content.on_watch:
🚨 VDR ISSUE RIGHT NOW?

1. VDR alarm/malfunction → report to Master and Chief Officer immediately
2. Sailing without working VDR → Port State Control deficiency, log it
3. After an incident → do NOT tamper with VDR

VDR capsule location: _____
ULB battery expiry: _____

topics.title: GMDSS
topics.subtitle: Global Maritime Distress and Safety System

content.quick_look:
A worldwide communication framework ensuring a ship in distress can send an alert and receive help — even with no crew interaction.

It's a system of equipment: DSC radio, EPIRB, SART, Navtex, and satellite comms.

⚠️ Most confusing part: The EPIRB must be registered with your flag state. An unregistered EPIRB wastes rescue time — authorities can't identify your vessel.
content.deep_dive:
GMDSS components:
• DSC — automated distress alerts on VHF Ch70, MF/HF
• EPIRB — activates manually or via hydrostatic release. Transmits on 406 MHz to COSPAS-SARSAT. Must be registered.
• SART — responds to 9 GHz radar pulses. Creates 12 dots on rescuer's radar.
• Navtex — automated receiver for navigational warnings on 518 kHz
• Inmarsat/Iridium — Satellite voice and data

Sea areas A1–A4 define which equipment is required.
content.on_watch:
🚨 DISTRESS SITUATION RIGHT NOW?

1. DSC distress → press red button, hold 3 seconds, follow prompts
2. VHF Ch16 MAYDAY → 'MAYDAY MAYDAY MAYDAY, this is [ship name] [MMSI] [position] [nature of distress] [persons on board] OVER'
3. EPIRB → ensure float-free bracket released
4. SART → hold at highest point, wait for radar response

NEVER test DSC distress without alerting coast guard first.

content.quick_look:
A unified control platform where navigation, communication, alarm management, and ship control systems are all connected through linked workstations.

⚠️ Most confusing part: The illusion of control. An IBS looks clean and organised, so people assume everything is fine. But if one sensor is faulty, all connected displays show wrong data confidently.
content.deep_dive:
All bridge equipment communicates through NMEA 0183, NMEA 2000, or Ethernet-based networks.

Key components:
• Conning display — speed, heading, RPM, rudder angle in one view
• AMS (Alarm Management System) — collects and prioritises alerts
• BNWAS — detects officer incapacitation, escalates alarm
• Autopilot — connected to ECDIS for track-following
• Engine telegraph — electronic, logged automatically

Alarm levels:
• Alarm (red) — immediate action
• Warning (yellow) — attention required
• Caution (cyan) — awareness
content.on_watch:
🚨 IBS PROBLEM RIGHT NOW?

1. Workstation frozen → switch to backup standalone instruments immediately
2. Alarm panel overwhelmed → address highest priority (red) first, never mass-dismiss
3. BNWAS alarm → reset immediately if you're operational
4. Data mismatch IBS vs standalone → trust standalone
5. Full reboot → inform Master, switch to manual watchkeeping

Standalone backup instruments: _____

content.quick_look:
Treats ballast water to kill invasive marine organisms before discharge in a different sea region. IMO Ballast Water Management Convention (2017) made it mandatory.

⚠️ Most confusing part: The paperwork. Port state control checks your Ballast Water Record Book intensely. Every ballast operation must be logged with position, volume, and salinity. Missing entries = immediate deficiency.
content.deep_dive:
Most BWTS use a two-stage process:

1. Filtration (40–50 micron) — removes larger organisms
2. Treatment:
   • UV radiation — damages DNA of organisms
   • Electrochlorination — generates chlorine from seawater
   • Chemical dosing — biocide injection

Discharge must meet D-2 standard:
• < 10 viable organisms per m³ (≥50 micrometers)
• < 10 viable organisms per mL (10–50 micrometers)

Log every uptake, treatment, and discharge.
content.on_watch:
🚨 BWTS ISSUE RIGHT NOW?

1. UV lamp failure → check hours, log malfunction
2. Pump rate exceeds treatment capacity → slow down ballast pump
3. Entering port with untreated ballast → contact Master immediately
4. Filter blocked → backflush procedure, check differential pressure
5. Port inspection → Record Book must be complete

Your system type: _____

content.quick_look:
Radar detects objects using radio waves. ARPA tracks those objects and calculates Closest Point of Approach (CPA) and Time to CPA (TCPA) automatically.

⚠️ Most confusing part: ARPA vectors are based on relative or true motion. In relative motion mode, a stationary buoy appears to move. Many near-misses have happened because an officer misread the motion mode.
content.deep_dive:
Radar bands:
• X-band (9 GHz) — higher resolution, better for collision avoidance
• S-band (3 GHz) — better in rain, longer range

ARPA data per target: true course, speed, CPA, TCPA, BCR, BCT

Vector modes:
• True motion — stationary objects appear stationary
• Relative motion — everything moves relative to own ship

CPA/TCPA:
• CPA < safe distance AND TCPA positive = action required
• TCPA negative = target has passed CPA

ARPA struggles with targets in sea clutter and fast-changing targets.
content.on_watch:
🚨 RADAR/ARPA ISSUE RIGHT NOW?

1. Target lost → manually check screen, re-acquire
2. CPA alarm → verify motion mode first, then take COLREGS action
3. Clutter obscuring targets → adjust carefully, don't over-suppress
4. ARPA vector seems wrong → newly acquired target, wait 3+ minutes
5. Radar frozen → reboot, switch to other radar as primary

Safe CPA: _____ nm
Safe TCPA: _____ min

content.quick_look:
Monitors, logs, and sometimes controls machinery — temperatures, pressures, RPMs, flow rates — from a centralised interface in the ECR.

⚠️ Most confusing part: Alert fatigue. Junior engineers start dismissing alarms without investigating — which is how small issues become catastrophic failures.
content.deep_dive:
Monitored parameters:
• Main engine — RPM, exhaust temps per cylinder, turbocharger speed
• Auxiliary engines — load, temperature, oil pressure
• Boilers — steam pressure, water level
• Tanks — levels and temperatures
• Cooling — sea water and fresh water temps

UMS requirements:
• Critical alarms transfer to duty engineer's cabin
• Dead man alarm — periodic acknowledgement required
• Automatic slowdown/shutdown for critical parameters

Alarm priorities:
• Shutdown — automatic stop
• Slowdown — automatic RPM reduction
• Alarm — engineer attention required
• Warning — pre-alarm
content.on_watch:
🚨 ENGINE ROOM ALARM RIGHT NOW?

1. Main engine oil pressure alarm → check gauge directly, do NOT dismiss
2. High exhaust temp single cylinder → check fuel injector, possible misfiring
3. Cooling water high temp → check sea water pump, strainer blockage
4. All alarms simultaneously → possible automation fault, check manually
5. UMS alarm in cabin → get to ECR immediately

Never silence an alarm you don't understand.

content.quick_look:
A computer-controlled system maintaining a vessel's position and heading using its own thrusters — without anchoring. Essential for offshore operations.

⚠️ Most confusing part: DP classes. DP1, DP2, and DP3 define redundancy level. Using a DP1 vessel for a DP2 operation is a serious safety and contract violation.
content.deep_dive:
Position reference systems:
• DGPS — more accurate than standard GPS
• Acoustic positioning — hydrophones tracking seabed transponders
• Taut wire — physical wire to seabed
• Fanbeam/Cyscan — laser-based reference

DP classes:
• DP1 — no redundancy, loss of position possible after single fault
• DP2 — single fault does not cause loss of position
• DP3 — full redundancy including A60 fire divisions

Control algorithm runs a Kalman filter — computing optimal thrust from noisy sensor data.
content.on_watch:
🚨 DP ALARM RIGHT NOW?

1. Position reference lost → check if other references still valid
2. Drive-off alarm → emergency stop thrusters, inform Master
3. Weathervane mode → vessel aligns to environment, clear the area
4. Any uncontrolled movement → report immediately, even with no damage
5. Power failure → DP may lose capability, manual thruster control

Minimum reference systems required: _____
DP class required: _____

content.quick_look:
Removes sulfur oxides (SOₓ) from engine exhaust — allowing the ship to burn cheaper high-sulfur fuel while complying with IMO 2020 (0.5% global sulfur cap, 0.1% in ECAs).

⚠️ Most confusing part: Open loop scrubbers are banned in many ports. Ships that forget to switch modes before entering a restricted zone face massive fines.
content.deep_dive:
Three types:
• Open loop — uses seawater, discharges washwater overboard
• Closed loop — recirculates treated water, stores residue onboard
• Hybrid — can switch between both

IMO compliant discharge limits:
• pH ≥ 6.5 (at 4m from discharge)
• PAH ≤ 50 μg/L
• Turbidity ≤ 25 FNU above ambient

Process: seawater or NaOH sprayed into exhaust. SO₂ reacts to form sulfurous acid, which is neutralised. Treated washwater discharged or stored.
content.on_watch:
🚨 SCRUBBER ISSUE RIGHT NOW?

1. Entering ECA or restricted port → switch to closed loop OR switch to compliant fuel
2. Washwater pH alarm → check caustic dosing or seawater alkalinity
3. High differential pressure → check nozzle blockage, soot accumulation
4. Bypass valve opened → log with reason, seal afterward
5. Port inspection → have scrubber data recorder printout ready

Your scrubber type: _____

content.quick_look:
The system that physically moves the rudder in response to helm commands from the bridge. On most commercial ships it's hydraulic — hydraulic fluid drives rams or rotary vanes connected directly to the rudder stock.

SOLAS requires two independent power units, regular testing before entering port, and a direct communication system between bridge and steering gear room.

⚠️ Most confusing part: The steering gear doesn't fail dramatically — it often fails gradually. Slow response, unusual noises, or hunting (rudder moving without command) are early warning signs most people ignore until it's too late.
content.deep_dive:
Two main types of hydraulic steering gear:

• Ram type — two or four hydraulic rams push the tiller arm connected to the rudder stock. Most common on larger vessels.
• Rotary vane type — hydraulic fluid acts on vanes inside a rotary actuator. More compact, used on smaller vessels.

Key components:
• Hydraulic power units (HPU) — pumps that pressurize the system. Minimum two required by SOLAS.
• Tiller — arm connecting the rams to the rudder stock
• Rudder stock — the shaft that physically turns the rudder
• Hunting gear — feedback mechanism that stops the rudder when it reaches the ordered angle
• Follow-up (FU) mode — rudder moves to and holds ordered angle
• Non-follow-up (NFU) mode — rudder moves only while control is held, no auto-stop
• Autopilot mode — rudder controlled automatically by autopilot system

SOLAS requirements:
• Steering gear test within 12 hours before departure
• Emergency steering drill every 3 months
• Direct communication between bridge and steering gear room
• Auxiliary steering gear capable of 7° per second movement
• Rudder angle indicator on bridge and at steering gear
content.on_watch:
🚨 STEERING GEAR ISSUE RIGHT NOW?

1. No helm response → switch to second hydraulic power unit immediately
2. Still no response → switch to NFU (Non-Follow-Up) control
3. NFU not working → switch to emergency local steering in steering gear room, inform Master
4. Rudder hunting (moving on its own) → disengage autopilot, switch to manual FU
5. Hydraulic fluid leak → inform Chief Engineer, reduce speed, prepare for emergency
6. Loss of steering in confined waters → anchoring may be required, sound appropriate signals

Emergency steering position: _____ (know this before you need it)
Steering gear room phone number: _____

content.quick_look:
A spinning gyroscope that finds true north — not magnetic north. Unlike a magnetic compass, it's unaffected by the ship's steel hull or electrical equipment.

It feeds heading data to ECDIS, autopilot, radar, and AIS. Every one of those systems is only as accurate as the gyrocompass.

⚠️ Most confusing part: Gyrocompass error. It's never perfectly zero. Every ship has a known gyro error — and if nobody corrects for it, your ECDIS shows you heading the wrong way. Check the error board before you trust the heading.
content.deep_dive:
A gyrocompass works on two physical principles: gyroscopic inertia (a spinning mass resists change) and precession (gravity acts on the tilt to align the spin axis to true north).

Key components:
• Gyro sphere — spinning rotor, typically at 6000–24000 RPM
• Binnacle/master compass — houses the gyro and electronics
• Repeaters — slave units showing heading at various bridge positions
• Follow-up system — transmits heading signal to connected equipment

Settling time after power-on: 1–4 hours. Never use gyro data immediately after startup.

Gyro error types:
• Speed error — caused by vessel's own speed, increases in high latitudes
• Latitude error — gyro is less accurate near the poles
• Rolling error — caused by heavy rolling in seaways

Always compare gyro heading to magnetic compass heading. Difference = gyro error ± variation ± deviation.
content.on_watch:
🚨 GYROCOMPASS ISSUE RIGHT NOW?

1. Gyro error suddenly changed → do NOT trust heading, compare to magnetic compass immediately
2. Gyro alarm → check if master compass is still running, report to Master
3. Total gyro failure → switch all systems to magnetic compass, reduce speed, inform Master
4. Large discrepancy between gyro repeaters → master compass may be hunting, check directly
5. After blackout recovery → gyro needs resettling time, do not trust for 1–4 hours

Known gyro error: _____ °
Magnetic compass location: _____

content.quick_look:
An electronic system that automatically steers the ship on a set course by continuously adjusting the rudder — so the officer of the watch isn't manually steering for hours at sea.

It takes heading input from the gyrocompass and rudder feedback from the steering gear to hold the course.

⚠️ Most confusing part: Autopilot is not a watchkeeper. COLREGS still require a proper lookout. Ships have struck objects while on autopilot because the officer assumed the system was handling everything. It's steering — not thinking.
content.deep_dive:
Autopilot modes:
• Heading control — maintains a fixed compass course
• Track control — follows a route programmed in ECDIS
• Auto/adaptive — adjusts sensitivity to sea conditions automatically

Key settings:
• Weather/rudder gain — how aggressively the rudder corrects
• Counter rudder — anticipatory correction before overshoot
• Yaw — acceptable deviation band before rudder activates

High gain = frequent small corrections (calm weather)
Low gain = fewer large corrections (rough weather, saves rudder wear)

Handover protocol:
• Disengage autopilot before entering TSS, port approaches, or restricted visibility
• Officer must be at helm before disengaging
• Check steering mode matches conditions before every watch
content.on_watch:
🚨 AUTOPILOT ISSUE RIGHT NOW?

1. Ship not holding course → check gain settings, sea conditions may need adjustment
2. Large course deviation alarm → take manual control immediately
3. Autopilot disengaged unexpectedly → take helm manually, check system fault
4. Track control deviation → verify ECDIS route is correct, switch to heading control
5. Approaching confined water → disengage to manual steering well in advance

Minimum advance notice before manual takeover: _____ minutes
When to switch to hand steering: _____

content.quick_look:
Measures water depth by sending an acoustic pulse downward and timing the echo from the seabed. Displayed as depth below keel or depth below transducer.

Critical in shallow water, port approaches, and anchoring operations.

⚠️ Most confusing part: Depth below transducer vs. depth below keel. The transducer is not at the keel — it's mounted higher. If you read 5m on the display and your draught is 6m, you're already aground. Always confirm which measurement the display is showing.
content.deep_dive:
How it works: Transducer mounted on the hull sends a 200 kHz (shallow) or 50 kHz (deep) pulse. Sound travels at ~1500 m/s in seawater. Return time ÷ 2 = distance to seabed.

Key settings:
• Draft offset — set correctly so display shows clearance under keel
• Range — auto or manual, adjust for shallow/deep water
• Alarm depth — triggers when depth drops below threshold

Factors affecting accuracy:
• Water temperature and salinity (changes sound speed)
• Aeration and bubbles under hull (causes false shallow readings)
• Rocky seabeds return strong echoes; mud returns weak ones

Dual frequency: 200 kHz for shallow/accurate, 50 kHz for deep water penetration.
content.on_watch:
🚨 ECHO SOUNDER ISSUE RIGHT NOW?

1. Shallow alarm → verify against chart depth, check draft, inform Master
2. No depth reading → check transducer for air bubbles, try alternate frequency
3. Erratic readings → vessel speed causing aeration, reduce speed
4. Depth shallowing rapidly → emergency stations, reduce speed immediately
5. Anchoring → monitor continuously, note holding ground type from chart

Safe under-keel clearance: _____ m
Draft offset setting: _____ m

content.quick_look:
Measures the ship's speed and distance travelled. Most modern ships use an electromagnetic or Doppler log. Speed data feeds into ECDIS for dead reckoning when GPS is unavailable.

⚠️ Most confusing part: Speed through water vs. speed over ground. The log measures speed through water — it doesn't account for current. A 2-knot current against you means your SOG is 2 knots less than your log speed. Dead reckoning using log speed alone in strong currents will put you in the wrong position.
content.deep_dive:
Types of speed logs:
• Electromagnetic log — probe extends below the keel, detects water flow. Accurate in shallow and deep water.
• Doppler log — sends acoustic beams to seabed or water layer. Gives speed over ground (bottom-track) or speed through water (water-track).
• GPS-derived SOG — not a true log, used as reference.

Key outputs:
• STW — Speed Through Water (log speed)
• SOG — Speed Over Ground (GPS)
• Distance log — cumulative nautical miles for voyage records

Log error: Biofouling on the sensor probe causes underreading. Clean regularly in dry dock.
content.on_watch:
🚨 SPEED LOG ISSUE RIGHT NOW?

1. Log reading zero → check probe is extended, check for fouling
2. Log and GPS SOG differ significantly → check for current or tidal stream
3. Log failure at sea → use GPS SOG for navigation, note in logbook
4. Dead reckoning needed (GPS failure) → use last known log speed and heading
5. Entering shallow water → switch to Doppler bottom-track if available

Log type on this ship: _____
Known log error: _____ %

content.quick_look:
The primary voice communication tool on the bridge. Used for ship-to-ship, ship-to-shore, and port communications. Range is roughly line-of-sight — 20 to 50 nautical miles depending on antenna height.

Channel 16 is the international distress, safety, and calling channel. It must be monitored at all times when at sea.

⚠️ Most confusing part: Channel discipline. Pilots, port control, and other ships all use different working channels. New officers call on the wrong channel or forget to switch back to Ch16 after a working channel conversation. Always return to Ch16.
content.deep_dive:
Key VHF channels:
• Ch16 — distress, safety, calling (always monitored)
• Ch13 — bridge-to-bridge navigation safety
• Ch70 — DSC digital only, no voice
• Ch12, 14 — port operations (varies by port)
• Ch06 — intership working channel

DSC (Ch70) sends automated digital alerts including your MMSI, position, and distress type — without speaking.

Output power:
• High (25W) — ship-to-shore
• Low (1W) — short-range intership

Radio checks must be done at start of every watch and logged.
content.on_watch:
🚨 VHF ISSUE RIGHT NOW?

1. No response on Ch16 → check squelch, check antenna, try handheld
2. Hearing distress call on Ch16 → listen, do not transmit unless you can assist, log it
3. Missed pilot call → try Ch16 then agreed working channel
4. Need to make distress call → 'MAYDAY MAYDAY MAYDAY, this is [name] [MMSI] [position] [situation] [POB], OVER'
5. Radio completely dead → use GMDSS handheld VHF from emergency equipment

Pilot channel for this port: _____
Port control channel: _____

content.quick_look:
Separates oil from bilge water so only clean water (below 15 ppm oil content) is discharged overboard. Required by MARPOL Annex I.

Every discharge must be logged in the Oil Record Book with position, volume, and OWS reading.

⚠️ Most confusing part: The magic pipe. Some crews bypass the OWS by discharging oily bilge directly overboard. This is a criminal offence. Port state control uses fluorescence testing and Oil Record Book audits to detect it. Officers have gone to prison for this.
content.deep_dive:
How it works (two-stage):

Stage 1 — Gravity separation: Oil rises because it's less dense than water. Large droplets separate in the coalescer chamber.

Stage 2 — Fine separation: Remaining emulsified oil passes through coalescing filter media.

15 ppm bilge alarm: Optical sensor monitors the output. If oil content exceeds 15 ppm, the overboard valve closes automatically.

MARPOL requirements:
• Discharge only when underway, >12nm from nearest land
• Oil content <15 ppm confirmed by approved monitoring equipment
• All operations logged in Oil Record Book Part I
• No discharge in special areas (Baltic, Mediterranean, etc.)

Sludge from OWS must be incinerated or landed ashore.
content.on_watch:
🚨 OWS ISSUE RIGHT NOW?

1. 15ppm alarm → overboard valve should auto-close, verify it did
2. OWS not reaching 15ppm → check filter element condition, replace if needed
3. Entering special area with full bilge → do NOT discharge, transfer to slop tank
4. Port inspection of Oil Record Book → every entry must match bilge pump logs
5. OWS bypassed by previous crew → report to Master and Chief Engineer immediately

Slop tank capacity: _____ m³
Oil Record Book location: _____

content.quick_look:
Generates steam used for heating fuel oil, cargo, accommodation, and sometimes driving turbines. On tankers especially, the boiler is critical — heavy crude cargo must be heated to flow.

⚠️ Most confusing part: Low water level is a boiler emergency, not just an alarm. If the heating element or furnace is exposed without water, it overheats and can rupture. A boiler explosion is one of the most catastrophic events in the engine room.
content.deep_dive:
Types of marine boilers:
• Auxiliary boiler — oil-fired, used for steam services
• Exhaust gas boiler/economiser — uses heat from main engine exhaust. Free steam when main engine is running.
• Composite boiler — both oil-fired and exhaust gas, automatically switches

Key components:
• Burner — atomises fuel oil and ignites it
• Furnace — combustion chamber
• Steam drum — collects and stores steam
• Safety valves — lift automatically if pressure exceeds maximum
• Gauge glass — direct visual water level indicator

Alarm hierarchy:
• Low water level warning → check feedwater pump
• Low-low water level → automatic burner shutdown
• High pressure → safety valve lifts automatically

Boiler water treatment: Chemical dosing prevents scale and corrosion. Test daily.
content.on_watch:
🚨 BOILER ALARM RIGHT NOW?

1. Low water level alarm → check gauge glass directly, do NOT trust display alone
2. Feedwater pump failure → start standby pump immediately
3. Low-low level (burner shutdown) → do NOT relight until water level is confirmed normal
4. Safety valve lifting → steam pressure too high, reduce firing rate
5. Flame failure alarm → check fuel supply, purge and relight following procedure
6. Unusual noise → inform Chief Engineer, reduce firing rate

Normal working pressure: _____ bar
Safety valve lift pressure: _____ bar

content.quick_look:
Treats sewage onboard so it can be legally discharged without polluting the sea. Required under MARPOL Annex IV.

Discharge of untreated sewage within 3nm of land is prohibited.

⚠️ Most confusing part: Many seafarers assume sewage doesn't matter compared to oil. PSC inspectors disagree — and the Sewage Record Book gets checked. Missing entries are an instant deficiency.
content.deep_dive:
Most common type: Biological treatment plant (activated sludge process)

Three stages:
1. Aeration chamber — bacteria break down organic waste using dissolved oxygen
2. Settlement chamber — treated solids settle out
3. Chlorination/disinfection — chlorine or UV kill remaining bacteria before discharge

Key parameters:
• Dissolved oxygen in aeration tank — bacteria need air to live
• Chlorine residual in discharge — must be within limits
• Sludge level — excess sludge must be removed

Discharge standards (MARPOL Annex IV):
• Coliform bacteria < 250 cfu/100ml
• Suspended solids < 35 mg/L
• BOD5 < 25 mg/L

Special areas (Baltic Sea): Zero sewage discharge unless plant has special certification.
content.on_watch:
🚨 SEWAGE PLANT ISSUE RIGHT NOW?

1. Alarm on panel → check which stage is faulting, refer to manual
2. Overboard discharge in restricted area → stop discharge immediately, hold in tank
3. Foul smell from plant room → check aeration blower, bacteria may be dying
4. Entering Baltic or special area → switch to holding tank mode
5. PSC inspection → Sewage Record Book must show all discharge positions logged

Holding tank capacity: _____ m³
Sewage Record Book location: _____

topics.title: Fire Detection System
topics.subtitle: Fixed Fire Detection and Alarm System

content.quick_look:
A network of detectors throughout the ship that identifies fire automatically and triggers an alarm at the central control panel — usually on the bridge or fire control station.

SOLAS requires fixed fire detection on all passenger ships and most cargo ships above 500 GT.

⚠️ Most confusing part: A zone alarm doesn't tell you exactly where the fire is — it tells you which zone. Each zone covers multiple compartments. You still have to physically investigate every space in that zone. Assuming it's a false alarm without checking has killed people.
content.deep_dive:
Detector types:
• Smoke detector (optical/ionisation) — installed in accommodation, ECR, cargo spaces
• Heat detector — fixed temperature or rate-of-rise, used in galleys and engine room
• Flame detector — infrared/UV, detects actual flame, used in machinery spaces
• Multi-sensor — combines smoke and heat, reduces false alarms

System components:
• Control panel — shows zone alarms, faults, and system status
• Detector loop — detectors wired in zones (typically 50 detectors per zone)
• Alarm sounders — bells/sirens triggered automatically
• Repeater panel — secondary panel in ECR or Captain's cabin

False alarm causes: Cooking steam, welding fumes, dust, aerosol sprays near detectors.

IMO requires: Weekly testing of one detector per zone on rotation. Results logged.
content.on_watch:
🚨 FIRE ALARM RIGHT NOW?

1. Note zone number on panel — do NOT silence yet
2. Send crew to investigate EVERY space in that zone
3. If confirmed fire → sound general alarm, inform Master, initiate muster
4. If false alarm confirmed → silence alarm, log cause, reset detector
5. Panel shows fault (not alarm) → report to ETO, check detector loop
6. Multiple zones alarming simultaneously → treat as real fire

Zone map location: _____

content.quick_look:
A fixed firefighting system that floods a protected space with CO₂ gas to extinguish fire by removing oxygen.

It works. But CO₂ is also lethal to humans — it displaces the oxygen needed to breathe. Releasing CO₂ into an occupied space kills everyone inside.

⚠️ Most confusing part: The two-step release. There's a pilot cylinder valve and a main bank valve — both must be operated in sequence. Activating only one does nothing. Panic and skipping a step means the system doesn't discharge and the fire keeps burning.
content.deep_dive:
System components:
• CO₂ cylinders — stored in the CO₂ room, usually 45kg each
• Pilot cylinder — small cylinder that opens the main bank valve
• Distribution manifold — routes gas to selected protected space
• Release cabinet — two-step controls with safety pins/covers
• Pre-discharge alarm — 20–30 second warning (time to evacuate)
• Ventilation shutdown — ventilation must be stopped before discharge

Release procedure (general — follow ship's specific SMS):
1. Confirm space is evacuated — headcount
2. Stop ventilation to space
3. Close all fire dampers
4. Sound alarm
5. Remove safety pin from pilot valve
6. Open pilot valve
7. Open main bank valve

After discharge: Do NOT re-enter without breathing apparatus. CO₂ is invisible and odourless.
content.on_watch:
🚨 CO₂ SYSTEM SITUATION RIGHT NOW?

1. Engine room fire, CO₂ needed → confirm ALL personnel out before release
2. Headcount not complete → do NOT release until confirmed
3. Accidental alarm/release signal → sound emergency, evacuate space immediately
4. After release → no entry without SCBA, maintain boundary cooling
5. CO₂ room door open with no reason → investigate, report to Master
